courseTitle: Layers and Object Properties — Complete Practical Guide
courseDescription: A comprehensive beginner-friendly course on AutoCAD layers and object properties. Learn how to organize drawings with layers, control line styles and thickness, manage object appearance, and create professional municipal drawings. This course emphasizes practical, hands-on skills required for real-world engineering work.
courseObjectives: Understand layers as the foundation of professional drawing organization
Create and manage layers for different drawing elements
Control line styles (solid, dashed, center lines) and line thickness
Edit object properties using the Properties panel and other tools
Fix objects placed on wrong layers using practical commands

phases.phaseDescription: Understand what layers are, why they are essential, and how professional engineers use them to organize drawings.

steps.title: What is a Layer in AutoCAD?
steps.subtitle: Think of layers like transparent sheets stacked on top of each other
steps.why: Without layers, all your drawing objects — walls, dimensions, text, doors — get mixed together into one confusing mess. Layers separate different types of drawing content so you can organize, control, and print each type independently.
instructions: Imagine you are creating a floor plan on transparent sheets
Sheet 1 has all the walls (WALL layer)
Sheet 2 has all the dimensions (DIMENSION layer)
Sheet 3 has room labels and text (TEXT layer)
Sheet 4 has door openings (DOOR layer)
You can show or hide any sheet without affecting the others
You can print any combination of sheets together
This is exactly how AutoCAD layers work

realWorldExample.description: A professional floor plan for municipality submission might have 10 to 15 layers: WALL (all walls), COLUMN (structural columns), DOOR, WINDOW, DIMENSION (all measurements), TEXT (room labels), GRID (column centerlines), HATCH (material fills), TITLEBLOCK, and more. Each layer controls a specific type of content.

note.label: Professional standard
note.text: Every municipal drawing submission requires properly organized layers. Drawings with random elements on Layer 0 are rejected by most municipalities as unprofessional.

steps.title: Layer 0 and Defpoints — Special Layers You Should Know About
steps.subtitle: Layer 0 is the default but should never be used for regular drawing
steps.why: Understanding these special layers prevents common beginner mistakes that corrupt drawings or create printing problems.
instructions: Layer 0: Every new AutoCAD drawing has this layer by default. It CANNOT be deleted or renamed. It is only for block geometry that needs to inherit layer properties. NEVER draw your actual walls, dimensions, or content on Layer 0.

Defpoints: AutoCAD creates this automatically when you place your first dimension. Dimension definition points are stored here. Objects on Defpoints are visible on screen but do NOT print. Never intentionally draw content on Defpoints.
Your drawing should have at least 8-10 named layers like WALL, COLUMN, DOOR, DIMENSION, TEXT, etc. All actual drawing content goes on these named layers, not on Layer 0.

commonMistake.consequence: When you try to change the appearance of all walls at once by changing the layer color, nothing changes because it is all on Layer 0. You must edit each wall individually.
commonMistake.solution: Always create named layers FIRST. Then draw on the correct layer.

note.label: Do not use Layer 0
note.text: Set your current layer to WALL (or COLUMN, DOOR, etc.) BEFORE drawing. Check the layer dropdown in the ribbon every single time before placing an object.

steps.title: The Three Key Layer Properties You Will Control
steps.subtitle: Color, Linetype, and Lineweight — the foundation of layer management
steps.why: These three properties control how every object on a layer looks. Understanding them now makes the rest of this course easy to follow.
instructions: Property 1 - Color: Each layer has a color (Red for WALL, Yellow for DIMENSION, etc.). Every object on that layer inherits this color. Changing the layer color instantly changes all objects on that layer.

Property 2 - Linetype: Controls whether lines appear solid, dashed, center-marked, or dotted. WALL layer = Continuous (solid). CENTERLINE layer = CENTER2 (dashed). This makes different elements visually distinct.
Property 3 - Lineweight: The printed thickness of lines. Walls print thick (0.40 mm). Dimensions print thin (0.18 mm). This creates a professional visual hierarchy on the printed drawing.

wall_layer.name: WALL
wall_layer.color: Red - so all walls are instantly recognizable as red
wall_layer.linetype: Continuous - walls are solid lines, not dashed
wall_layer.lineweight: 0.40 mm - walls print thick and prominent

note.label: You control all three
note.text: When you create a layer, you assign all three properties. Once set, every object on that layer automatically gets these properties. This consistency makes drawings look professional and professional, and it makes editing fast.

phases.phaseTitle: Creating and Managing Layers in Layer Properties Manager
phases.phaseDescription: Hands-on work creating the layer structure for a municipal residential drawing.

steps.title: Open Layer Properties Manager and Create Your First Layer
steps.subtitle: Command: LA or LAYER
steps.why: This is where you create, name, color, and configure all layers for your drawing. This single dialog is your layer control center.
instructions: Type LA in the command line and press Enter
The Layer Properties Manager panel opens on the left side of your screen
You see Layer 0 already listed — this is the default layer
Click the New Layer button (page icon with a star at the top of the panel)
A new layer appears in the list named Layer1
Double-click Layer1 name and type WALL to rename it
Press Enter to confirm the rename

firstLayerSteps.action: Double-click the name 'Layer1' and type WALL
Click the Red color square next to WALL layer, select Color 1 (Red) from the dialog
Click 'Continuous' next to WALL layer, select Continuous from the dialog
Click '0.30 mm' next to WALL layer, select 0.40 mm for walls

note.label: Keep Layer Manager open
note.text: Do not close Layer Properties Manager while you are creating all your layers. Keep it open in the panel on the left so you can create layers one after another quickly.

steps.title: Create All Standard Layers for Municipal Drawings
steps.subtitle: Build your complete layer structure in 10 minutes
steps.why: Professional drawings require a standard set of organized layers. Creating all layers before drawing anything ensures a clean, consistent drawing structure.
instructions: Keep Layer Properties Manager open
Create each layer by clicking New Layer, renaming it, then setting its color, linetype, and lineweight
Follow the Standard Layer Table below for each layer
For any linetype other than Continuous: click the linetype cell, click Load if the linetype is not listed, find it in the library, and click OK
Do not skip any layer — all are needed for complete professional drawings

standardLayers.name: CENTERLINE
standardLayers.use: Column grid lines and center references

note.label: CENTER2 linetype loading
note.text: If CENTER2 is not in the linetype list, click Load, find CENTER2 in the acadiso.lin library, and click OK. This linetype is standard but must be loaded once.

steps.title: Set the Current Layer Before Drawing Each Element
steps.subtitle: Critical habit: Check the layer dropdown before placing any object
steps.why: The current layer is where all new objects will be placed. Drawing walls on the TEXT layer or dimensions on the WALL layer creates chaos and wastes hours fixing it later.
instructions: Look at the Layer dropdown in the ribbon at the top left of the screen
It shows which layer is currently active
BEFORE drawing walls — click the dropdown and select WALL
BEFORE adding dimensions — click the dropdown and select DIMENSION
BEFORE typing room labels — click the dropdown and select TEXT
Make this a habit: Always check the layer dropdown and set the correct layer BEFORE placing any object

steps: Look at the Layer dropdown in the ribbon (top-left of screen)
Select the layer you want to draw on
Your new objects will now be placed on that layer

note.label: This single habit prevents 90% of layer problems
note.text: Professional engineers check the current layer every single time before drawing. This is not extra work — it is fundamental discipline that saves hours of rework.

phases.phaseTitle: Understanding Line Styles — Linetypes and Thickness
phases.phaseDescription: Learn how to control the appearance of lines: which lines are solid, which are dashed, and how thick each line prints.

steps.title: Linetype Basics — Solid Lines vs Dashed vs Center Lines
steps.subtitle: Why different elements need different line styles
steps.why: Different drawing elements need different line styles to be visually distinct. Walls are solid. Center lines are dashed. Hidden elements are dotted. This visual distinction makes drawings clear and professional.
instructions: Continuous (solid) — walls, doors, windows, all permanent building elements. These lines are completely solid with no gaps.
CENTER2 (dashed pattern) — grid lines, column centerlines, reference lines. These lines have a pattern of dashes and gaps that makes them visually distinct.
DASHED — hidden lines, elements behind a cut plane. Used in sections and elevations.
HIDDEN, PHANTOM, DOT — other specialized linetype patterns for specific situations

layers.reason: Walls are permanent features shown as solid lines
Grid lines are reference only, shown as dashed for distinction
Dimension lines are measurements, shown solid

note.label: Linetype is a layer property
note.text: You assign a linetype to a layer when you create it. All objects on that layer inherit the linetype. If all walls are on the WALL layer with Continuous linetype, all walls automatically appear as solid lines.

steps.title: Lineweight — Controlling How Thick Lines Print
steps.subtitle: Professional drawings have a visual hierarchy: thick lines, thin lines, very thin lines
steps.why: Lineweight creates visual hierarchy. Important elements like walls print thick. Details like dimensions print thin. This makes the drawing clear and easy to read when printed on paper.
instructions: Lineweight is how thick a line appears when printed on paper
It does NOT affect how the line looks on screen by default — only on the printed output
To see lineweights on screen while working: Type LWDISPLAY and press ON (or click the LWT button in the status bar)
Standard lineweights for municipal drawings:
— 0.09 mm: Very thin — grid lines, reference lines
— 0.18 mm: Thin — dimensions, text, light lines
— 0.25 mm: Medium-thin — doors, windows, secondary elements
— 0.40 mm: Medium — standard walls
— 0.50 mm: Thick — columns, heavy structural elements
— 0.70 mm: Very thick — section cut lines, title block border
— 1.00 mm: Extra thick — drawing border frame

structure.reason: Important but secondary to walls — printed thinner

note.label: Lineweight is assigned per layer
note.text: Just like color and linetype, you assign lineweight to a layer when you create it. All objects on that layer use the same lineweight. This ensures consistency: all walls print at 0.40 mm, all dimensions at 0.18 mm.

steps.title: LTSCALE — Controlling the Size of Dashed Line Patterns
steps.subtitle: If center lines look like solid lines, increase LTSCALE
steps.why: Sometimes a dashed linetype appears as a solid line on screen because the dashes are too small to see. LTSCALE controls the overall size of the dashed pattern.
instructions: Type LTSCALE and press Enter
The command line shows the current value, usually 1
For 1:100 scale drawings, try LTSCALE = 75 to 100
For 1:50 scale drawings, try LTSCALE = 50 to 75
Type the new value and press Enter
Look at your center lines — if the dashes are now visible, you have set LTSCALE correctly
If dashes are too large, decrease LTSCALE. If they are too small, increase it.

visualGuide.problem: CENTER2 linetype appears as a solid line
visualGuide.cause: LTSCALE is too low — the dashes are smaller than screen pixels and appear solid
visualGuide.solution: Type LTSCALE 100 and press Enter. The dashes are now larger and visible.

note.label: LTSCALE is drawing-wide
note.text: LTSCALE affects ALL dashed linetypes in your entire drawing. Set it once to a value that looks good for your drawing scale. For 1:100 drawings, LTSCALE = 75-100 is typical.

phases.phaseTitle: Object Properties — Making Changes to Individual Objects
phases.phaseDescription: Learn how to edit the properties of specific objects using the Properties panel and other tools.

steps.title: Open Properties Panel to Edit Any Object
steps.subtitle: Ctrl+1 — the most powerful tool for fixing objects
steps.why: The Properties panel lets you see and edit every property of any selected object: layer, color, linetype, lineweight, size, position. It is the fastest way to fix objects.
instructions: Select any object on your drawing by clicking it
Press Ctrl+1 — the Properties panel opens on the right side showing all properties of that object
You can now edit any property:
— Change the Layer: click the layer name and select a different layer
— Change the Color: click the color value and select a new color
— Change the Linetype: click the linetype and select a different one
— Change Geometry: edit exact coordinates, length, radius, etc.
Press Enter to apply any change

commonFixes.problem: I drew a wall on the wrong layer
commonFixes.solution: Select the wall, press Ctrl+1, click Layer field, select WALL, press Enter. Done.

commonFixes.problem: A dimension is in the wrong position
commonFixes.solution: Select it, press Ctrl+1, edit the X and Y coordinates to the correct position, press Enter
Select it, press Ctrl+1, find the Height property, type a larger value, press Enter

note.label: Properties panel is faster than dialogs
note.text: Using Properties panel to change an object's layer or color is faster than selecting the object, right-clicking, and using a dialog. Ctrl+1 is your speed shortcut.

steps.title: ByLayer vs Explicit Properties — Understanding Property Inheritance
steps.subtitle: Professional objects are set to ByLayer, not explicit colors
steps.why: When an object is set to ByLayer, it automatically inherits the color, linetype, and lineweight of its layer. If you later change the layer properties, all objects on that layer update automatically. Explicit properties override this and cause inconsistency.
instructions: ByLayer (correct): Object uses color, linetype, and lineweight from its layer. If you change the WALL layer color from Red to Blue, all objects on the WALL layer become Blue instantly.
Explicit/Override (incorrect): Object has a specific color, linetype set directly on it. If you change the WALL layer color to Blue, this object stays Red because it has an explicit override.
Professional practice: Always set object properties to ByLayer. This ensures consistency and makes editing fast.
To set an object to ByLayer: Select it, press Ctrl+1, set Color to ByLayer, Linetype to ByLayer, Lineweight to ByLayer

example.scenario: You drew 50 walls and want to make them all thicker for printing
withByLayer: 3. Check: Color = ByLayer, Linetype = ByLayer, Lineweight = ByLayer
4. If yes, all 50 walls are set to inherit from WALL layer
5. Open Layer Properties Manager, click WALL layer
7. All 50 walls instantly become thicker — no need to select each one
withExplicitProperties: If each wall was drawn with an explicit lineweight (not ByLayer):
You must select each of the 50 walls individually and change them one by one
This takes 10 minutes instead of 10 seconds

note.label: Best practice is always ByLayer
note.text: Professional engineers set all object properties to ByLayer unless there is a very specific reason to override. This maintains consistency and makes editing efficient.

steps.title: Match Properties — Copy Properties from One Object to Others
steps.subtitle: Command: MA (MATCHPROP) — the fastest way to fix objects on wrong layers
steps.why: If you have 10 walls drawn on the wrong layer, selecting each one individually and changing it is slow. Match Properties lets you fix all 10 at once.
instructions: Type MA and press Enter
Click a correctly placed object as the source — this is the object whose properties you want to copy
The cursor changes to a paintbrush
Click all the incorrectly placed objects to transfer properties from the source
Press Escape or Enter to finish
All selected objects now have the same layer, color, linetype, and lineweight as the source

practicalExample.problem: You drew 10 walls but they are all on Layer 0 instead of WALL layer
solution: 1. Draw one wall correctly on the WALL layer
3. Click the correctly placed wall as source
4. Click each of the 10 incorrectly placed walls
5. All 10 walls are now on the WALL layer

timeComparison.slow_way: Select each wall individually, press Ctrl+1, change layer manually — 10 minutes
timeComparison.fast_way: Type MA, click source, click 10 walls — 30 seconds

note.label: Match Properties is your speed tool
note.text: Every professional uses MA regularly. Instead of fixing each object individually, fix one correctly, then copy its properties to all the broken ones. This is the fastest way to fix layer organization mistakes.

phases.phaseDescription: Learn quick commands that let you control layers without opening menus and dialogs.

steps.title: Quick Layer Commands — Freeze, Isolate, Lock Without Dialogs
steps.subtitle: Speed-essential layer management keyboard shortcuts
steps.why: Opening the Layer Properties Manager every time you need to hide a layer is slow. These commands let you control layers instantly from the drawing.
instructions: LAYISO (Layer Isolate): Make only one layer visible
Command: Type LAYISO, press Enter, click any object on the layer you want to isolate
Result: All other layers become invisible, only your selected layer is visible
Use: When you want to focus on editing walls without other elements distracting
LAYFRZ (Layer Freeze): Freeze a layer instantly
Command: Type LAYFRZ, press Enter, click any object on the layer to freeze
Result: That entire layer becomes invisible and locked from memory
Use: Freeze completed layers like TITLEBLOCK to prevent accidentally editing them
LAYOFF (Layer Off): Hide a layer instantly
Command: Type LAYOFF, press Enter, click any object on the layer to hide
Result: That layer becomes invisible but still in memory
Use: Quickly hide furniture or hatch to see the structure clearly
LAYLCK (Layer Lock): Lock a layer so nothing can be selected
Command: Type LAYLCK, press Enter, click any object on the layer
Result: Objects on that layer cannot be selected or moved
Use: Lock WALL layer after drawing walls to prevent accidentally moving them while adding dimensions

workflowExample.task: Draw dimensions on a floor plan without accidentally moving walls
steps: 1. After finishing all walls, type LAYLCK and press Enter
3. All walls are now locked — they cannot be selected or moved
4. Now draw dimensions freely without fear of moving walls
5. When done with dimensions, type LAYULK and click any wall to unlock

note.label: These commands are much faster than the dialog
note.text: Instead of: Open Layer Manager, find the layer, click the icon — you just type LAYISO and click an object. Professional engineers use these keyboard shortcuts constantly.

steps.title: Select Similar — Select All Objects of One Type at Once
steps.subtitle: Right-click > Select Similar — instantly select all walls, all doors, etc.
steps.why: Instead of selecting objects one by one, Select Similar lets you select all walls, all dimensions, or all doors on a layer instantly. Then change all their properties together.
instructions: Click any wall object
Right-click and choose Select Similar from the context menu
All objects on the WALL layer are now selected
Now you can:
— Change their layer all at once (press Ctrl+1, change layer field)
— Change their color all at once
— Delete them all at once
— Move them all at once
This works for any layer: click a door, Select Similar, all doors are selected

practicalExample.problem: You have 20 dimension lines and want to move them all to a different location
practicalExample.slowWay: Click each dimension, move it manually — very tedious
fastWay: 4. Press Move (M), specify a vector to move all 20 at once

note.label: Select Similar is perfect for batch operations
note.text: Whenever you need to change multiple objects of the same type, use Select Similar. It saves huge amounts of time.

steps.title: Layer States Manager — Save and Restore Complete Layer Configurations
steps.subtitle: Save a 'working state' and a 'printing state' — switch between them instantly
steps.why: While working, you might want all layers visible. But when printing, you want only specific layers to print. Layer States let you save both configurations and switch between them with one click.
instructions: Type LAYERSTATE and press Enter — the Layer States Manager opens
Click New to save the current layer configuration with a name
Name it WORKING_STATE (all layers you normally work with visible)
Click OK
Now turn off all layers except those you want to print
Click New in Layer States Manager again
Name it PRINT_STATE
Click OK
Now whenever you need to print:
Type LAYERSTATE, select PRINT_STATE, click Restore
Only your print layers are visible and will print
After printing, do the same: restore WORKING_STATE to go back to full view

workingState.name: WORKING_STATE
workingState.visibility: All layers ON — you see everything while drawing

printState.name: PRINT_STATE
printState.visibility: Only structure layers (WALL, COLUMN, DOOR, WINDOW, DIMENSION) are ON. Construction layers like GRID and CONSTRUCTION are OFF

note.label: Professional workflow uses Layer States
note.text: Every professional AutoCAD user has multiple layer states saved. Switch between them instantly: one for working, one for printing, one for review. This prevents mistakes in printing.

phases.phaseDescription: Quick reference for the most frequent layer and property issues and their solutions.

steps.title: Problem: Walls Changed Color After I Changed the Layer Color
steps.subtitle: This is actually CORRECT behavior — objects set to ByLayer inherit layer colors
steps.why: Understanding this prevents you from 'fixing' something that is already correct.
instructions: What happened: You had walls on the WALL layer set to ByLayer. You changed the WALL layer color from Red to Green. All walls turned Green instantly.
Why: When an object is set to ByLayer, it automatically inherits color from its layer. This is intentional and correct.
Is this a problem? NO — this is exactly how ByLayer is supposed to work. This is why professionals use ByLayer.

If you do NOT want walls to change when you change the layer color, select each wall, press Ctrl+1, set the color to a specific value like Red (not ByLayer). But this defeats the purpose of using layers.

note.label: ByLayer is working as designed
note.text: This is not a bug or problem — it is the entire point of using ByLayer. Objects that inherit from their layer automatically update when the layer is edited.

steps.title: Problem: Center Lines Look Like Solid Lines on Screen
steps.subtitle: Increase LTSCALE — the dashes are too small to see
steps.why: For high zoom levels or small-scale drawings, the dashes in a CENTER2 linetype may be smaller than the screen pixels and appear as solid lines.
instructions: Type LTSCALE and press Enter
Type a larger value — try 100 for 1:100 scale drawings
Press Enter
Look at your center lines — dashes should now be visible
If still solid, increase LTSCALE more (try 150). If dashes are too coarse, decrease it.
For 1:50 drawings, try LTSCALE 50-75
For 1:100 drawings, try LTSCALE 75-100
For 1:200 drawings, try LTSCALE 150-200

note.label: LTSCALE is a global setting
note.text: LTSCALE affects all dashed and center line patterns in your entire drawing. Set it once to a value that looks good, then leave it.

steps.title: Problem: I Cannot Select or Move an Object
steps.subtitle: The layer is probably locked — use LAYULK to unlock it
steps.why: When a layer is locked, all objects on it cannot be selected or edited. This is a safety feature to prevent accidentally moving completed elements.
instructions: Type LAYULK and press Enter
Click the object you cannot select
The layer containing that object is now unlocked
Try selecting and moving it again — it should work now
If you want to lock a layer again after editing:
Type LAYLCK and press Enter
Click any object on the layer to lock it

note.label: Check layer lock status
note.text: Before concluding something is broken, always check if the object's layer is locked. Look at the Layer Properties Manager — a locked layer has a padlock icon.

steps.title: Problem: Objects Are Placed on the Wrong Layer
steps.subtitle: Use Match Properties (MA) to fix all of them at once
steps.why: It is easy to accidentally draw on the wrong layer. Instead of moving each object individually, Match Properties fixes all of them instantly.
instructions: Step 1: Draw ONE object correctly on the right layer (e.g., one wall on the WALL layer)
Step 2: Type MA and press Enter
Step 3: Click the correctly placed object as the source
Step 4: Click all the incorrectly placed objects
Step 5: Press Escape — all objects are now on the correct layer
Example: You drew 15 dimension lines but they are all on the WALL layer
1. Draw one dimension correctly on the DIMENSION layer
2. Type MA, click it as source
3. Click all 15 incorrect dimensions
4. All 15 are now on the DIMENSION layer

note.label: Match Properties is the fastest fix
note.text: Never manually fix 10+ objects. Use Match Properties every time.

phases.phaseTitle: Professional Checklist — Before Printing Your Drawing
phases.phaseDescription: Final verification steps to ensure your drawing is organized, consistent, and ready for municipal submission.

steps.title: Layer Organization Checklist
steps.subtitle: Verify every layer is correct before printing
steps.why: Municipal authorities reject drawings with poor layer organization. Following this checklist ensures your drawing meets professional standards.
instructions: Open Layer Properties Manager (type LA)
Check: Do all required layers exist? ✓ WALL ✓ COLUMN ✓ DOOR ✓ WINDOW ✓ DIMENSION ✓ TEXT ✓ CENTERLINE ✓ HATCH ✓ GRID
Check: Each layer has a unique color assigned (RED for WALL, YELLOW for DIMENSION, etc.)
Check: Each layer has the correct linetype (WALL = Continuous, CENTERLINE = CENTER2)
Check: Each layer has the correct lineweight (WALL = 0.40 mm, DIMENSION = 0.18 mm)
Check: Objects are on the correct layers — use Select Similar to verify

note.label: Layer organization is non-negotiable
note.text: Professional drawings MUST have organized layers. Disorganized layers = drawing rejected by municipality.

steps.title: Set Print State and Verify Print Visibility
steps.subtitle: Before printing, ensure only the correct layers are visible
steps.why: You do not want construction lines, reference layers, or partially completed elements to print. Layer States ensure only final, approved content prints.
instructions: Open Layer States Manager (type LAYERSTATE)
If you have not already, save a PRINT_STATE configuration with only printable layers visible
Restore PRINT_STATE before printing
Layers that SHOULD print: WALL, COLUMN, DOOR, WINDOW, DIMENSION, TEXT, CENTERLINE, HATCH
Layers that SHOULD NOT print: GRID (reference), CONSTRUCTION (temporary lines), VIEWPORT (layout viewports)
Verify by looking at the drawing — only finalized content is visible

note.label: Print state prevents mistakes
note.text: Accidentally printing construction lines or grid references is a common mistake. Using a dedicated PRINT_STATE prevents this.

steps.title: Final Verification Checklist — Ready to Print
steps.subtitle: One final review before sending to municipality
steps.why: This checklist catches the most common problems that cause drawings to be rejected.
instructions: ✓ All layers are created with correct names, colors, and linetypes
✓ All objects are placed on the correct layers
✓ All objects are set to ByLayer (not explicit properties)
✓ Lineweights are set per layer: walls thick, dimensions thin, grid very thin
✓ LTSCALE is set correctly so center lines are dashed, not solid
✓ Text height is consistent (all room labels same size, all dimension text same size)
✓ Dimensions are on the DIMENSION layer in yellow color
✓ PRINT_STATE is active — only printable layers are visible
✓ Drawing title block is complete with project name, scale, date, engineer name
✓ Print preview shows exactly what will print on paper
beforePrinting: 1. Restore PRINT_STATE from Layer States Manager
2. Use File > Print Preview to see exactly what will print
3. Check that no unwanted layers are visible
4. Check that line thickness looks professional (thick walls, thin dimensions)
6. Only then proceed to actual print

note.label: This checklist prevents 99% of print problems
note.text: Professional engineers complete this checklist every single time before printing. It takes 2 minutes and saves hours of reprinting mistakes.
